name: Bassett's Quarry tracksite
short_description: A historic quarry site in the Connecticut River Valley known for Mesozoic-age mineral specimens and dinosaur track fossils. Limited public access, but offers a unique geological window into the reg...
description:
Bassett's Quarry tracksite is located in Easthampton within the Connecticut River Valley, a region renowned for Mesozoic age sedimentary rocks and fossil-bearing strata. The site sits on moderate terrain at 523 feet elevation, characteristic of the valley's gently rolling topography. This location is notable as a historical source for dinosaur ichnofossils (trackways) and associated mineral specimens from the Triassic and Jurassic periods, when this area was an active rift basin.

The geology here reflects the early breakup of Pangaea, when the Atlantic rift zone created a series of grabens and half-grabens across what is now New England. These basins accumulated thick sequences of red beds, sandstones, and siltstones interbedded with volcanic material. Quartz, feldspar, and mica are the dominant minerals found in the metamorphic and sedimentary rocks exposed at the quarry. The site's fossil heritage makes it scientifically significant, though collection regulations are strict.

Access to Bassett's Quarry is limited and often restricted due to safety concerns and site protection protocols. Rockhounders should verify current access conditions with local authorities before visiting. The moderate slope terrain requires sturdy footwear and careful footing around exposed rock faces and quarry edges. The site appeals primarily to mineral enthusiasts and geology students interested in studying New England's unique Mesozoic rift basin deposits rather than casual collectors.

When access is permitted, focus on studying the quarry wall exposures where weathering has freed mineral specimens from the host rock. The surrounding area may yield loose fragments of feldspar-rich material and quartz chips. Always exercise caution around active or inactive quarry operations, as unstable rock faces and deep pits present serious hazards.
history: Bassett's Quarry sits within the Connecticut River Valley rift basin, a Mesozoic-age structure that formed during the initial rifting of the Atlantic Ocean approximately 200 million years ago. The quarry has historical significance as a source of Triassic-Jurassic dinosaur trackways and associated mineral specimens, making it important to both paleontology and geology education in Massachusetts.
tips: Verify access permission before visiting, as the site is often restricted due to fossil protection and quarry safety regulations. If access is granted, examine the quarry walls and freshly exposed rock faces where feldspar and quartz crystals are most visible. Bring a hand lens to identify individual mineral grains in the sandstone and siltstone matrix, and wear a hard hat if entering active quarry zones. Look for white to pink feldspar crystals and transparent quartz fragments in the coarser-grained sandstone layers.
terrain_notes: Moderate slope terrain at 523 feet elevation with exposed quarry faces and sedimentary rock outcrops. The ground surface is uneven with loose rock fragments scattered around the quarry perimeter. Walking distance from parking is typically short but depends on current site configuration. Footing is rough and potentially unstable near quarry edges; sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support are essential.
written_directions: From Easthampton town center, take Route 10 north approximately 1.5 miles. The quarry site is located on the east side of Route 10 near the valley floor. Local landmarks and property boundaries are not clearly marked from public roads, so contact the town of Easthampton or Massachusetts Department of Geological Services for precise directions and current access information. GPS coordinates are 42.230606, -72.65892.
road_conditions: Route 10 is a maintained state road with standard pavement. Access roads to the quarry site may be gravel or unmaintained.
parking_notes: Parking at this site is extremely limited and access is typically by prior permission only. No established public parking area exists.
collection_rules: Access to Bassett's Quarry tracksite is restricted and requires advance permission from the property owner or the town of Easthampton. Fossil collection is strictly prohibited. No motorized excavation. If collecting minerals is permitted by the property owner, collection is limited to small loose specimens. No hammering on quarry walls or excavation of embedded material.
rules:
Advance permission required for site access
Fossil collection strictly prohibited
No motorized equipment
No hammering or excavation of quarry faces
Small loose specimens only if collecting is permitted
No commercial collection
Respect all posted signs and barriers
nearest_services: Easthampton center, approximately 1.5 miles, has gas stations, restaurants, and a pharmacy.
accessibility_notes: Moderate slope terrain with uneven, rocky footing and loose rock scattered throughout. No wheelchair access. Quarry edges present cliff hazards. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or fear of heights. Sturdy footwear and careful attention to footing required at all times.

faq.question: Can I visit and collect minerals at Bassett's Quarry tracksite in Easthampton?
faq.answer: Bassett's Quarry is privately owned and not open to the general public. Access requires advance permission from the property owner or the town of Easthampton. Fossil collection is strictly prohibited to preserve the site's paleontological significance. If access is granted, only small loose mineral specimens may be collected with the owner's permission. Contact the Easthampton town office or Massachusetts Department of Geological Services for current access information.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Bassett's Quarry in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: The primary minerals at Bassett's Quarry are quartz, feldspar, and mica, sourced from Triassic-Jurassic age sandstones and siltstones. Feldspar occurs as white to pink crystals visible in the coarser-grained rock layers. Quartz appears as transparent to translucent fragments and small crystals. The site is equally noted for its Mesozoic dinosaur trackways, which are protected and cannot be collected.

faq.question: Is there a permit or fee to rockhound at Bassett's Quarry tracksite near Easthampton?
faq.answer: Bassett's Quarry requires advance permission for access, though there is no standard public permit system. The site is private property, and rockhounders must contact the property owner or Easthampton town officials. There is no established fee, but collection privileges depend entirely on the owner's consent. Fossil collection is never permitted regardless of access status.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Bassett's Quarry in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: The best season to visit Bassett's Quarry is May through September when weather is mild and the terrain is most accessible. Winter conditions can make the site slippery and hazardous due to exposed rock and quarry edges. Spring and early summer offer the most favorable conditions for examining exposed rock faces.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Bassett's Quarry tracksite?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens or magnifying glass to identify feldspar and mica crystals in the host rock. Wear sturdy hiking boots with ankle support and bring a hard hat if entering active quarry zones. A small collection bag for loose specimens and a camera for documentation are useful. Do not bring hammers or digging tools, as excavation of quarry faces is prohibited.

faq.question: Is Bassett's Quarry tracksite in Easthampton suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Bassett's Quarry is not recommended for beginners or families due to restricted access, moderate terrain with loose rock hazards, and proximity to quarry cliffs. The site requires advance permission, has no established public facilities, and is primarily of interest to geology students and experienced collectors familiar with site protocols and safety requirements. Families should explore other more accessible rockhounding sites in Massachusetts.

meta_description: Explore Triassic-Jurassic quartz, feldspar, and mica at Bassett's Quarry in Easthampton, MA. Private access only. Dinosaur trackway geology.
